title: BRCA1 and PARP inhibitors in breast cancer therapy
abstract: BRCA1 is a tumor suppressor gene involved in DNA damage repair through homologous recombination. Mutations in BRCA1 are strongly associated with hereditary breast and ovarian cancers. PARP inhibitors such as Olaparib exploit synthetic lethality in BRCA1-mutated tumors by blocking alternative DNA repair pathways. Clinical trials have demonstrated that Olaparib improves progression-free survival in patients with BRCA1-mutated breast cancer. Other PARP inhibitors including Niraparib and Rucaparib are also being investigated for BRCA1-deficient tumors.

title: TP53 mutations and their role in cancer
abstract: TP53 is a crucial tumor suppressor gene that encodes the p53 protein, a transcription factor regulating cell cycle arrest, apoptosis, and DNA repair. TP53 is the most frequently mutated gene in human cancers. The p53 protein interacts with MDM2 in a negative feedback loop that regulates p53 stability. Mutations in TP53 are associated with Li-Fraumeni syndrome and a wide spectrum of cancers including breast, lung, colorectal, and ovarian cancers. The PI3K/AKT pathway can also influence p53 activity through phosphorylation.

title: EGFR signaling pathways in non-small cell lung cancer
abstract: Epidermal Growth Factor Receptor (EGFR) is a transmembrane receptor tyrosine kinase that regulates cell proliferation, survival, and differentiation. EGFR mutations are common driver events in non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC). Tyrosine kinase inhibitors such as Gefitinib and Erlotinib target mutant EGFR and are used as first-line therapy for EGFR-mutated NSCLC. EGFR signaling activates multiple downstream pathways including the RAS/RAF/MEK/ERK and PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathways. Resistance to EGFR inhibitors often arises through secondary mutations such as T790M in the EGFR kinase domain.
